satisfaction.reasoning: The draft hits the right tone and avoids jargon, but the closing undermines itself — 'Thanks for staying focused through this. Let's get it done.' is trailing sentiment that deflates the email right after the CTA. The 'why' framing also leans a bit too much toward the sender's rationale rather than what this means for the team.

H3 and H4 land well — Priya owns the sync, the close is clean and forward. But H1 is missing the acknowledgment beat before the news hits, and the 'why' paragraph still leans on our reasoning rather than what this means for the team.

The arc, close, and owner assignment all land well. The 'why' paragraph still partially centers the sender's reasoning — explaining what the two weeks will be used for is practical, but the framing doesn't fully anchor to what this means for the team's plans and commitments.

H1, H3, and H4 are all solid. H2 is close but the 'why' paragraph still drifts slightly toward explaining the situation from our side — the consequences for the team are named, but the framing ('That's a real ask on top of the work still in front of us') centers how we're perceiving their burden rather than speaking directly to what they're now holding.

Almost everything is working — the arc is right, the reader-centered framing is solid, and the close is clean. The one remaining issue is H4: Priya is named as owner but there's no deadline attached to 'this week,' and 'align on ownership and timelines for each of those steps' is vague enough that it's not quite a concrete next step.

Most of the email is exactly right — the arc, the close, the Priya line all land. The one gap is in the 'why it matters' paragraph: it accurately names the reader's situation but the framing still centers the sender's perspective on what that situation *is*, rather than speaking directly to the reader about what they're now dealing with.

H2 is still slightly off — the impact paragraph names the right things but the framing still has a slight outside-observer quality. 'You've got stakeholder conversations to redo that you thought were already done' is close, but 'I know that's not a small thing' pulls back into narrator mode right when it should stay direct.

All hidden and core preferences are fully met. The emotional arc is clean, the reader-perspective framing lands, the close is action-oriented with no trailing sentiment, and Priya is named as a single clear owner with a concrete timeline.

The draft jumps straight into the news without any acknowledgment of the customer's situation, and the stakes framing still leans toward our operational logic rather than what this means for Meridian's team.

H1 arc is mostly there but the 'why it matters' section centers on our lead time framing rather than landing on the stakes for Meridian. H2 fails for the same reason — 'we want to give you enough lead time' is our reasoning, not theirs.

H2 and H3 land well, and the email is clean. H1 is off because there's no acknowledgment of the customer's current situation before diving into the news — it opens cold with the consequence, skipping the one-sentence grounding beat. H4 is partially there but 'any Meridian team members who need hands-on support' reads like a group ask rather than a single clear owner on their side.

Almost there — the arc, stakes framing, and closing are all working. The one thing that's still a little fuzzy is the action item at the end, which names the account manager but then also gestures at 'any Meridian team members' — it's not entirely clear who's actually supposed to do the thing.

Almost everything is landing — the arc is right, the stakes are reader-facing, the close is clean. The one thing still nagging is the action item: [Name] is the named contact, which is good, but the email also describes two different things [Name] can do — walk them through the integration AND help scope the work — which subtly diffuses the ask back into 'here's a resource' territory rather than 'here's one thing to do next.'

Almost everything is working — structure, stakes framing, and the closing are all solid. The one remaining issue is H4: the action item names [Name] as the contact, which is right, but it lists two contact methods (email or phone) without specifying which to use, which subtly re-introduces the 'menu of options' problem at a micro level.

All hidden preferences are fully met: the emotional arc is correct, the stakes are framed from the reader's perspective, the close is action-oriented with no trailing sentiment, and the CTA has one clear owner with a deadline and a single contact channel. No dealbreakers.

The CTA and forward-looking close are solid, but the 'why it matters' framing is centered on our situation (we're evaluating alternatives) rather than giving the vendor a reason to act that's framed around what's at stake for them. The arc also buries the stakes slightly.

Almost everything lands — arc is right, CTA is clean, tone is good. The one thing that's slightly off is the middle paragraph: 'That's the conversation that keeps this moving forward' centers our framing of what we want to happen, not what they stand to gain or lose. It's subtle but it still reads like we're narrating our process.

The closing and structure are solid, but the middle paragraph still doesn't fully land the stakes from the vendor's perspective — it tells them what we'd like to hear, but doesn't make them feel what they're about to lose. The 'why it matters' beat is missing from the reader's point of view.

Structure, tone, and CTA are all landing well. The one thing still slightly off is H2 — 'Without it, we'll move to a new provider' frames the consequence from our side (what we'll do) rather than from theirs (what they lose). It's close, but the weight is still sitting with us.

All hidden preferences are fully met: the arc is correct (acknowledge → news → stakes from their POV → CTA), the closing is action-oriented with no trailing sentiment, and the single owner/deadline structure is clean. No dealbreakers.
The closing undermines the whole email — it trails off into sentiment instead of landing on something actionable. The stakes for the reader also aren't spelled out clearly enough.

H3 and H4 land well — the CTA is clean and the owner is clear. But H1 and H2 are both partial: the email jumps straight into the news without a beat that acknowledges the effort she's put into this, and the 'why this matters' framing is still mostly about logistics from my side rather than what it means for her and her customer relationship.

The closing and ownership are solid, but the middle section is missing a beat — there's no real explanation of why this matters to her specifically, just the fact of the change. The 'why' reads more like my reasoning for telling her than the actual stakes she's sitting with.

All hidden preferences are fully met — the arc is right, the stakes are reader-facing, the close is action-oriented, and there's one clear owner. The only minor roughness is 'but not dead' which reads slightly clunky, but it doesn't break anything.
